Figure 3: Sparsification error curves for the semantic and spatial probabilities for Swin-T

Table 4: Ablation study on deterministic and Bayesian deep learning techniques. We compare final performance and uncertainty calibration across the different uncertainty estimation methods (MC-Dropout, Mask-Ens, Deep-Ens, Snap-Ens) and the respective deterministic version. We also report results for two different backbone configurations Resnet-50 and Swin-T.

Table 5: Ablation study on backbone architectures. We compare final performance and uncertainty calibration across three encoder backbones: ResNet-50 (R50), ResNeXt-101 (Rx101), and Swin-T. For each backbone, we report results for both the deterministic model and its Bayesian counterpart based on Snapshot Ensembles (Snap-Ens).

Table 6: Ablation study of sampling locations for uncertainty estimation. We report calibration (AUSE, ECE) and performance (Fw β , pPMQ) metrics for different sampling configurations applied to Swin-T using MC Dropout and Mask Ensembles.
Table 1: Affordance segmentation comparative with the state-of-the-art in the IIT-Aff dataset.
Table 2: Affordance segmentation comparative with the state-of-the-art in the UMD dataset.
Figure 4: Qualitative results and uncertainty prediction obtained by the Swin-T Mask-Ens Bayesian Instance segmentation model

Figure 1: Our model architecture is composed of an attention-based backbone [13] extended with sampling layers [14, 15]. We compare the performance and calibration of different sample-based (MC-Dropout [14], Mask-Ens [15]) and ensembles (Deep-Ens [16], Snap-Ens [17]) approaches for the Bayesian inference.
Table 3: Per-class Fw β (↑) affordance segmentation scores on the IIT-Aff test split dataset.
